title: Blindsight
phenomenon: Some people with damage to primary visual cortex can discriminate stimuli in the affected field above chance while denying or lacking ordinary visual awareness.
adversarialPrompt: Explain preserved visually guided discrimination without ordinary reported visual awareness in blindsight. Distinguish absent experience from degraded experience, conservative report criteria, and inaccessible information.
rationale: Performance and reported awareness dissociate, forcing a theory to identify which processes remain and which proposed condition for consciousness is missing.
assumptions: Forced-choice performance is above chance and not driven by cues outside the affected field.
Awareness reports and discrimination responses can be measured independently.
knownConfounds: Conservative report criteria can resemble absent awareness.
Residual or degraded vision can be misclassified as no experience.
Eye movements and intact visual islands can support performance.
targetCommitments: Which processing supports discrimination without ordinary awareness.
Which proposed condition for consciousness is absent.
How the account separates absent, degraded, and inaccessible experience.
changeMindObservation: The theory's proposed awareness condition is present while carefully controlled discrimination persists without any theory-predicted awareness witness.
